jokes.text: Hi, I'm Jerry Seinfeld. I'm moving in. / Saw your name on the buzzer. You must be Kessler. / No, actually, it's Kramer. / You need any help or...? / No, thanks. / I ordered a pizza, you want some? / No, no, no. I couldn't impose. / Why not? / We're neighbours. What's mine is yours.
jokes.setup: The very first meeting of Jerry and Kramer — Jerry moving in, calling him 'Kessler' (the original name from early Seinfeld).
jokes.punchline: 'Why not? / We're neighbours. What's mine is yours.' — Kramer immediately establishing his lifetime philosophy of treating Jerry's apartment as his own, in their very first exchange.
jokes.explanation: The episode's most extraordinary comedic moment and structural achievement. The backward episode concludes at the very beginning — Jerry and Kramer meeting for the first time. 'Kessler' references the original Seinfeld pilot, a deep meta-reference. But the real joke is that Kramer immediately, in their FIRST conversation, establishes 'what's mine is yours' — the philosophy that has defined nine seasons of boundary-violation. The entire series is implied and justified in one exchange. The character integration score approaches perfection: this is the ur-Kramer moment. Rewatch bonus is essential — this moment becomes more meaningful with full series context.

jokes.text: Oh, hi. I'm Jerry Seinfeld. I'm moving in. Saw your name on the buzzer. You must be Kessler. / No, actually, it's Kramer.
jokes.setup: The episode ends at the very beginning — Jerry is just moving into the building, meeting Kramer for the first time.
jokes.punchline: Jerry calls him 'Kessler' (the name that was used in the original pilot before the character was developed) — and Kramer corrects him.
jokes.explanation: The episode's crown jewel. This final scene — Jerry and Kramer meeting for the first time — is the ultimate payoff of the reverse-chronology structure. The use of 'Kessler' (Kramer's name from the unaired pilot) is a meta-reference that rewards superfans while working as a straightforward character beat for casual viewers. The entire episode has been leading backwards to this origin moment. Structurally, this is exceptional television writing. The meta-joke about Seinfeld's own production history is perfectly woven into the narrative. Earns the highest score of the episode.

jokes.text: I ordered a pizza, you want some? / No, no, no. I couldn't impose. / Why not? / We're neighbours. What's mine is yours.
jokes.setup: Kramer and Jerry have just met — they're strangers.
jokes.punchline: Kramer immediately offers 'What's mine is yours' to a complete stranger — which we know is also the template for Kramer's entire relationship with Jerry (constantly taking from Jerry's apartment) but stated here as a generous opening offer.
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect character-origin joke. Kramer's 'What's mine is yours' to a stranger is immediately funny, but knowing the full series, this is the founding document of their relationship — except Kramer has it backwards (it's always what's JERRY's that is Kramer's). The irony is complete and the character integration couldn't be better. Worthy of 8+.

jokes.text: Good night, Jugdish.
jokes.setup: The episode's final line — Kramer using 'Jugdish' as a farewell to Jerry.
jokes.punchline: In their very first meeting, Kramer already calls Jerry 'Jugdish' — which has been established as Jerry's name in Indian. This makes no sense in context, making it the perfect absurdist closer for the backward episode.
jokes.explanation: Perfect closer. 'Jugdish' has been the episode's running gag, seeded in the India sections; now it appears in the very first Jerry-Kramer conversation — which makes no literal sense and is absolutely the right choice. The absurdist non-sequitur as a final line, after the emotional weight of seeing their first meeting, is tonal perfection. Economy score of 10: one word, maximum impact, closes the episode.

tmdb_overview: Flashbacks trace the evolution of Jerry, George and Elaine's disastrous trip to a wedding in India, and Kramer's desperate need to barter for a birthday wish.
insight: Reverse-chronological structure collapses under weak material, delivering just 2.3 jokes per minute.
